turns.text: So, on the day that the Nazi Party came to power, you took over the task of building a Nazi air force, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, not an air force. The immediate problem was the linking up of all the various branches of aviation which existed at that time. For instance, there was one civil aviation transport company, or there might have been two. There were the aviation industries, the training schools for civilian pilots, the meteorological service, and I believe there were several research institutes. That, I think, covers the entire field of aviation of that time -- but it had nothing to do with an air force.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Perhaps, I will say, you took over the task of making Germany predominant in the air?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I cannot agree with that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Put it in your own way. Tell us what you did; what your object was in taking over this new task.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: My first task was to develop the various branches in order to build up a large air transport system.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You then made visits to France and England, and on your return reported to Hitler personally, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When you returned from England, did you warn Hitler against the activities of Ribbentrop?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What did you tell Hitler about the activities of Ribbentrop in England?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That I had gained the impression in England that Von Ribbentrop was not persona grata .

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now,when you were interrogated before, didn't you state after your capture that you told Hitler that if he did not get rid of Ribbentrop soon he was going to have trouble with England? Is that not what you told Hitler in substance?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot now remember the exact words.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But is that not the sense of it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I was of the opinion that another man should be sent to England to bring about mutual understanding as to policy, in accordance with the wish so often expressed by Hitler.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Before you talked with Hitler about that, you had discussed it with Goering, had you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: With whom?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Goering.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: About the journey? Or about what?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: About Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I did not discuss him with the Reich Marshal.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: There came a time when some engineers were sent to Russia, were they not, to inspect the air construction there, factories, facilities, and that sort of thing?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, that is correct.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: This was a group of engineers, and you had something to do with sending them there, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I had nothing to do with that group. At that time technical research was not under my control.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Under whose orders were they?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Under General Udet, who, in turn, was under the Reich Marshal.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And when they came back, you learned that they had reported that Russia had greater capacity for building airplane engines than all six of the German factories, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, that is correct.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What order did Goering give about that information being made available even to the Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Goering did not believe the information at that time. I know that from the words of General Udet.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Is it not a fact that you stated to the interrogators before that Goering called these experts defeatists, forbade them to repeat that information to anybody, and threatened them with the concentration camp if they repeated that information? Did you say that or didn't you?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I never said it in that form.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, use your own words and tell us just what Goering said on that subject.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: At a considerably later date, when the question of American armament figures came Up, the Reich Marshal said to me, "Now, you too are going to turn defeatist and believe these large figures." I told him then that I did indeed believe these figures; but that had nothing to do with the Russian matter.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were those Russian figures ever reported to Hitler, to the Reichstag or in any way made public to the German people?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The Russian figures? That I cannot say. I had nothing to do with the matter. The American figures were undoubtedly submitted to Hitler, but Hitler did not believe them.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You testified on Friday, I believe, that you knew that the commencement of the war with Russia would mean the annihilation of Germany. I remind you of that, and that is correct, is it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Not the destruction -- the defeat. I think I said annihilation or defeat.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You went to Reich Marshal Goering to protest against the entrance into the Russian war, is that right?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did Goering agree with you that it would end in the defeat of Germany?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, he did not agree. He had to be extremely cautious in his statements in deference to his relations with Hitler. I told him the cause for Germany's difficulties and he nodded. His words gave me the impression that he had already put the same arguments to Hitler, and that he had been unsuccessful.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: In other words, he agreed with you that it would end with the defeat of Germany, but did not want it said to Hitler, is that right?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I would not go as far as that. When I said that this meant the defeat of Germany, I was voicing the conclusion reached by me. He merely agreed that this war should be avoided at all costs and that it would prove a misfortune for Germany. That was the way he put it; he did not use the word "defeat" in this connection.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Was it mentioned by you?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I mentioned that to open a second front against so strong an enemy would mean the defeat of Germany.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And did he disagree with you about that? Did he take issue with you about that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, he did not argue about it, he only declared himself opposed to taking on anything else, as he considered it impossible to do so; what we thought would not make the slightest difference and it would only give Hitler the impression that we in the Luftwaffe were defeatists.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you did not attempt any further to convey the information, from which you thought Germany would be defeated if she entered into war with Russia, to Hitler or to any other officer of the High Command?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: It was impossible for me to do so. I could not act against the order of my superior officer.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Of the Reich Marshal?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, of the Reich Marshal.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And, so far as you know,after his talk with you he never conveyed the information to Hitler that it was your opinion that the war would end in disaster?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I had the impression that he had previously discussed the subject with Hitler but without any degree of success, because with Hitler that was impossible.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, but you had been abroad for Hitler and reported to him and he apparently had confidence in you, and I am asking you if Hermann Goering ever reported to Hitler that you, from your information, felt that it was a disaster to go into that war?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: My trips were not made at Hitler's order. They were made in response to invitations from foreign governments to the Luftwaffe and at the order of the Reich Marshal. It was only because I was aware of the dmportance of these trips and because I incidentally heard political statements -- in spite of my reluctance at the time, since they did not concern me as a soldier -- that I thought it my duty to report personally to Hitler.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did Goering direct you to do that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: To go to Hitler? Yes, Goering told Hitler about it and Hitler ordered me to report to him. I myself did not say, "I am now going to see Hitler," but I received an order to that effect from Hitler himself.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And he did not send you to Hitler until he knew what you were going to report?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, he himself had...

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So he did know?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: He himself had no cognizance of the subject. He had no time to receive me.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Goering had no time to receive you?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. Goering at that time had many other matters on hand and he did not want to hear about these things.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So he left that to Hitler, who was not busy, I take it. Is that true?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Hitler was interested in the matter.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I think you told us in interrogations that Goering was not very industrious. Is that correct?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I should be very reluctant to answer that question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Very well, I withdraw it. It was not a kindly question to begin with. When you found that Germany was going into a war which you, an informed officer, considered a disaster, did you resign?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Resign? What from?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Resign your commission as an officer or take any other steps to protest?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, that was absolutely impossible. There was an order which ruled it impossible.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And who gave that order?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Hitler himself.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, you said you had experienced this yourself.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Not only in my own case. The order applied generally.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You said on Friday that you experienced it yourself, that you could not resign.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No; one could not resign.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you try it at any time?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I frequently applied for my discharge in peacetime. My resignation, however, was not accepted, the reason given being that I had no right to ask for it, but that I would be told by higher authorities when I had to go. During the war I never applied for my discharge, because as a soldier in wartime I could not apply for it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you not have some talk with Goering at one time about retiring from your position, in which he not only forbade you to leave, but also told you there would be no use in feigning ill health?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes. There was no possibility of giving this as the reason unless one was really ill. When retiring from a high position it had been customary in the past to plead ill health. Now this was no longer possible.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And he did suggest to you in that discussion one way out, did he not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, he did not suggest a way out, but I did.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What did you suggest? What talk did you have about suicide? Did Goering tell you that the only way you could get out was to commit suicide?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That would have been the only possible way out.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, did Goering tell you that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I said that; not he.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And he did not disagree with you, I take it.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. He did not care if I did or not.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, you have the regulations with you, which you say were printed for the information of every soldier, about international law and regulations. You have them with you this morning?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I have them with me; the regulations are contained in my service book, the same as for every soldier.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You gave us a little information about that, but I would like you to get that out and give us exactly the text of those instructions or regulations, which you say reflect international law as you understood it.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Do you want me to read it out now? The quotation ...

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Not too fast.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. "Ten Commandments for the Conduct of the German Soldier in War. "1. The German soldier fights chivalrously for the victory of his people. Cruelty and needless destruction are unworthy of him. "2. The fighter must wear a uniform, or else he must be provided with insignia visible from a good distance. Fighting in civilian clothes without such insignia is prohibited. "3. No enemy once he has surrendered shall be killed, not even a partisan or a spy. The courts will administer the just punishment. "4. Prisoners of war must not be maltreated or insulted. Weapons, plans and notes are to be taken from them. Apart from these, none of their possessions may be taken from them. "5. Dum-dum bullets are prohibited. Bullets may not be transformed into dum-dum bullets. "6. The Red Cross is inviolable. Wounded enemies must be treated humanely. Medical orderlies and chaplains must not be hindered in the performance of tfieir medical and spiritual functions. "7. The civilian population is inviolable. The soldier must not plunder or wantonly destroy. Historical monuments and buildings dedicated to religious service, art, science, or charity must be treated with special care. Personal services and services in kind shall only be required of the civilian population against compensation, and if ordered by the superior officer. "8. Neutral territory must not be militarily involved by trespassing, by planes flying over it, or by gunfire. "9. If a German soldier is captured, he must state his name and rank when questioned. Under no circumstances may he say to what unit he belongs, or speak about military, political, or economic conditions on the German side, neither may he allow himself to be induced to do so by threats or promises. "10. Any contravention of these orders while on active service is punishable. Breaches by the enemy of the rules listed under 1 to 8 are to be reported. Reprisals are permissible only by order of the higher commanders."

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now that, as you understand it, is the military law conforming with international law, which was promulgated for the governance of the troops in the field?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you understood, and it was generally understood in the German Army, that that was international law, was it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Every soldier could not help knowing that these were the German regulations because they were pasted on the first sheet of the pay book, issued to every soldier, and which he had to carry on him. The common soldier, of course, did not know that they represented international law.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The higher commanders, like yourself did, didn't they?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That represented your understanding and interpretation of your duties and obligations as honorable men in combat?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, did you participate in the activities of Hermann Goering in collecting the art treasures of France and other occupied territories?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you participate in the removal of the civilian population for forced labor?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You know that was done, do you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I did not know that the workers who came from foreign countries had been deported; we were told that they had been recruited on a voluntary basis. In the case of France, I know that up to a certain date the French had wanted to come, but after that date they no longer wanted to come, and that the French Government itself had issued directives to deal with this.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Aside from that, then, you did not know anything about involuntary or forced labor in Germany? Is that your testimony?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. I only knew that ...

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Tell us what you did know about and what you did about it.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I knew that those people had been recruited and that they had come voluntarily. I knew that many of them were very satisfied, but as time went on and the German military situation deteriorated, discontent began to set in among these foreign workers, although, according to the information which reached my ears, only a small group was affected. I would add that in a general way, we ascribed this ill feeling to the fact that the food for these people was not everything they could wish; consequently, sundry organizations, with Speer's ministry at the head, made efforts to improve their living conditions.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You have not yet answered my question. Did you know that forced labor was being brought from occupied territories and compelled to work in German industry? Did you know it? Answer that "yes" or "no."

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I knew that only in the end Frenchmen were forced by their own French Government to come.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you know that prisoners of war were forced to work in the airplane industry, and were actually forced to man guns? Did you know that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I did hear about it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you heard about it from your fellow officers, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: At the moment I cannot say from whom I heard it. I believe there was a group which I think was called "Volunteers." As far as I know it was recruited on a voluntary basis from among those brisoners of war. MR, JUSTICE JACKSON: And did you learn about -- even if you did not participate in it -- the plan for the collection of art treasures from the occupied countries?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. I knew nothing of this plan as it then existed. I first heard about it here in Nuremberg through someof the witnesses. MR, JUSTICE JACKSON: Now I want to ask you some questions about certain exhibits; I refer to Document Number 343-PS, Exhibit USA-463. I will ask to have that exhibit shown to you. [Document 343-PS was subraitted to the witness.]

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: These letters are signed by me and they are also written on my stationery. They must have been drafted by the Medical Inspection department. As I said a few days ago, I no longer remember the contents. I should only like to say that the answers were drafted in such a way as not to lead us, the Air Force, into any difficulties with Herr Himmler. For instance, I never read the statements made by Dr. Rascher and Dr. Romberg. They were read by the Medical Inspectorate. In this connection I acted, so to speak, as postman between the SS and our Medical Inspection department.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When you testified, on interrogation, you had no recollection of these letters; but on Friday you testified that you made some alterations in one of them before it went out. Do you want to tell us what that alteration was?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, some of these letters were submitted to me during my interrogation and it was then that I first remembered it. The changes which I made were merely a matter of courtesy in style, in view of Herr Himmler's extreme susceptibility. I do not think that either of these two letters contains the alteration; that, I believe, was in another letter.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It was the other letter in which there was a change, Number 1607?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I believe so, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, in your examination, your interrogation, you gave a reason why these were brought to you for signature instead of being signed by the bureau chiefs. Do you remember what that reason was?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes. I had the impression that the Medical Inspector did not wish to address his refusal to Himmler because he was afraid; whereas Himmler had written to me because he always wrote only either to the Reich Marshal or to me, as he was unacquainted with the organization of the Luftwaffe in this particular sphere, for the Medical Inspector was not subordinate to me.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, I understand from your interrogation that you gave as the reason why these letters were brought to you for signature, that your office was in fear of Himmler and did not want to take the responsibility of writing a letter to him, is that right?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Not my office, but I think the Medical Inspection department did not wish to place themselves in an awkward position as concerns Himmler.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And I think you also said that the officials of that department were afraid of the SS.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That is what I wished to express.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were they engaged in any illegal conduct or any activity against the government?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I did not understand that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Were those people who were afraid ...

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Who? The Medical Inspection department? No.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: They were responsible officials doing their duty, as far as you know, is that right?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice; but one must bear in mind the things which had come to pass during the war.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is exactly what I want you to think about and tell about. Why were these people, who were performing their duties in a government office, afraid of Himmler or afraid of the SS? Explain that situation to us.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Not afraid of the SS as such, but of the secret police. It was not easy for any of us. We were all convinced that we were being constantly watched, no matter how high our rank. There was probably not a single person concerning whom a dossier was not kept, and many people were subsequently brought to trial as a result of these records. The ensuing difficulties did not affect only these people or other people or me personally; they included everybody right up to the Reich Marshal, who also was affected by them.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So you mean that from the Reich Marshal right down to the humblest citizen, there was fear of Heinrich Himmler and his organization?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Well, the degree of fear may have varied. It was perhaps not so great among those in the highest and in the lowest positions. But things were far more difficult in the intermediate grades, since it was quite clear that the intermediate grades criticized everything that occurred and these criticisms were not tolerated by the authorities at the top.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I take it, from your testimony, that the reputation of the Gestapo was pretty well understood in Germany.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Particularly so in the later war years. I could not say how far this feeling was justified, but at all events the feeling was there.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, I think you also testified that some high military authorities did resign. I call your attention to your testimony in your interrogation by us about Von Fritsch and Beck. They resigned, didn't they?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, they did not resign. They were removed.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: They were thrown out, is that it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes. They were told they were no longer needed.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I understodd you to testify in your interrogation that even the generals did not dare utter an opinion after those two left.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I never put it like that. I cannot remember what I said. I should be grateful if I could see the minutes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, I have them. I will ask you if you were not asked these questions and gave these answers: "Question: From your knowledge of discussions in army circles among the Air Force and the General Staff people whom you knew, could you form any opinion as to their attitude for the beginning of war? Would they share your view?" The minutes show that you answered: "All officers agreed with me unanimously. All the higher officers agreed with me. A long time ago, in 1937, 1 talked to Field Marshal Von Blomberg about the danger of a war because of the careless policy of our statesmen. At that time we feared that England or France would not tolerate that policy in the long run. On the 1st of November 1937, 1 had a long discussion with Von Blomberg about this matter, and he was of the same opinion."

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I remember.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is true? You were then asked this question:

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, they had always been subordinate. The Army was always changed in this respect. The head of the State was at the same time the Supreme Commander.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: At the time you were interrogated, your answer was this:

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, that was my conviction.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: [Continuing.] Is that true?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That is my opinion.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: [Continuing.] Is that right?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Correct.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And of what were the high generals in command of the Army afraid, that they did not utter an opinion?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The generals would not have had a chance to report anything to Hitler.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who would have done anything about it? There were many generals and only one Hitler. Who was going to carry out any orders against them?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: It was just not possible. Hitler was so powerful that he just turned down other people's objections or else refused to listen to them at all.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Hitler had the SS, didn't he, and Himmler and Kaltenbrunner?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, he had them as well. In addition he had the entire Wehrmacht who had sworn an oath of allegiance to him.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I think you said in your interrogation that after the 5th of March 1943, Hitler was no longer normal. Did you make that statement?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I said that, in my opinion, the Hitler of the later years was not the Hitler of the early period from 1933 until the outbreak of war, and that after the campaign against France a change came over him. I formed this opinion, which was a purely private one, because what he did afterwards was diametrically opposed to what he had previously taught; and that I could not consider normal.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you want us to understand that Goering continued to act as second man in the Reich and to take the orders from an abnormal man from that period on? Is that your story?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The abnormality was not such that one could say, "this man is out of his senses," or, "this man is insane"; it would not have to reach that stage. It often happens that abnormalities are such that they escape both the public and the nearest associates. I believe that a doctor would be better able to give information on that subject. I talked to medical men about it at the time.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And it was their opinion that he was abnormal?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That there was a possibility of abnormality was admitted by a doctor whom I knew well, personally.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: A doctor of repute in Germany?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, he is not very well known. He never told anybody else. It would not have been wise to do so.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: If he had, he would have been put in a concentration camp, I suppose?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Or worse..

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And if you had expressed your opinion that he was abnormal, you probably would have been put there also, would you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I would have been shot immediately.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: So you never dared to tell your superior, Goering, your opinion about Hitler?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I only once had an opportunity of stating my views about the war to Hitler. That was the only time.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You informed Goering of your opinion?

turns.speaker: MIILCH
turns.text: I talked to Goering. What I have just mentioned was a conversation I had with Hitler.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, you do not -- I think you misunderstood me -- you do not mean that you informed Hitler that you considered him abnormal; I am sure you do not mean that.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I did not tell Goering that either.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is what I said. You knew, did you not, that Goering, who was your immediate superior, was issuing the anti-Jewish decrees of the Reich Government?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I did not know that. As far as I know, they emanated from a different office, from ...

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Didn't you know that the decrees which excluded Jews and half-Jews from holding posts were issued by Goering?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I did not know that. As far as I know, these regulations emanated from the Ministry of the Interior, which also would have been the proper department to deal with them.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: As a matter of fact, did you not have to take certain proceedings to avoid the effect of those decrees yourself?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. I know what you mean. That was a question that had been cleared long ago.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How long before that was it cleared?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: As far as I know, in 1933.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: 1933, just after the Nazis came to power?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And at that time Goering had you -- we will have no misunderstanding about this -- Goering made you what you call a full Aryan; was that it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not think he made me one; I was one.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, he had it established, let us say?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: He had helped me in clearing up this question, which was not clear.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is, your mother's husband was a Jew; is that correct?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: It was not said so.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You had to demonstrate that none of your ancestry was Jewish; is that correct?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes; everybody had to do that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And  -- in your case that involved your father, your alleged father;  -- is that correct?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you, certainly were informed from the very beginning of the attitude of the Nazi Party to Jews, were you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I was not informed. Everybody had to submit his papers, and the certificate of one of my grandparents could not be found.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you were never required to do that under the Weimar Republic?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, there was no such question at that time.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you knew that this whole question was raised by the Nazi Party, of which you became a member in 1933; in other words at about the time this happened. Is that right?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I had applied for membership earlier, before this question came up.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When did you apply for membership?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not know exactly --  think in March or April.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you had to clear up this question before you could become a momber; wasn't that the point?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That had been cleared up in the meantime. I cannot say exactly when.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: In 1933 you became aware of the concentration camp, the first one?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I believe in 1933 there was a public announcement about it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And later, as I understand you, you heard so many rumors about concentration camps, that you thought the matter ought to be investigated; that you ought to go there and see?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When was it that these rumors became so persistent that you thought the matter should be investigated?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That must have been at the end of 1934 and in the spring of 1935, because, if I remember correctly, I was in Dachau in the spring of 1935.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And those rumors persisted throughout the entire period until the collapse of Germany, didn't they?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Those rumors which led me to ask to visit Dachau were really only current in the circle of the higher officers, who passed them on to me. I had little contact with other circles; I cannot say to what extent the thing was generally discussed.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, among the higher officers with whom you associated, the rumor went about that these concentration camps were the scene of atrocities as early as 1935. 1 understood you to say that; am I correct?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, not exactly. I said there ...

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, now you tell us what it was that you went to investigate.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I was quite unable to conduct any investigation; all I could do was to see for myself -- in order to dispel the many rumors -- whether it was true that many people were shut up there who should not have been there at all, innocent people who were brought there for political reasons only. At that time there was much talk about many members of the so-called "Reaction" having been sent there. Some officers were very concerned about this, and I told them that I would go and see for myself to try to gain a personal insight.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You did not need to go to Dachau to find that out, did you? You could have asked Goering; didn't you know that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: To go where?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you ever ask Goering who were these people who were sent there?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. I did not talk to Goering about that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you not know that Goering publicly said that political enemies of the regime were going to be sent there; that was what they were founded for; did you know that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot say I ever heard that that had actually been said, but that was what I surmised at the time, and I wanted to see for myself.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you found nobody there except criminals?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: All that I was shown were people who had committed crimes or rather serious offences. The only political prisoners I saw were people who had taken part in the Rohm Putsch. Whether there were others, I am unable to say, because I cannot swear that I saw the entire camp. But we saw all we asked to see. We said, "Now I would like to see this, or that," and the guide took us there.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: By whose authority did you get into the concentration camp for an examination?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Himmler's.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who asked Himmler if you could go?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not understand.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did Goering know that you were making the trip?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not think so. I did not make a special trip. I had some business in southern Germany in my military capacity, and I set aside one morning for this purpose.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: There were people in the concentration camp who had to do with the Rohm Putsch, as you call it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How many were there who had to do with that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot say exactly. As far as I remember now, I should say that altogether I saw about four or five hundred people.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Four to five hundred people; and how many were killed?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Well, I could not be too sure about this figure, there might easily have been 700. 1 estimate it at around that figure.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: How many people were killed in the Rohm Putsch?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I can only give the figure which Hitler publicly stated in the Reichstag; I cannot say from memory. I may be right if I said the number ranged between 100 and 200.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now why were you so concerned about the concentration camps? Did you have any official responsibility for them?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I had no responsibility whatsoever; but there was so much talk about them at the time that I decided I would find out for myself. I knew how many questions would be asked me, and I would not be able to answer them; so I said I would go there and see for myself.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, Germany had ordinary prisons for criminal prisoners, had she not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Of course.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And those prisons had sufficed for a good many years to take care of the criminal population, had they not? MILCH, I could not say what their purpose was.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And the concentration camp was something new that came in after 1933?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes. It is true I never heard of anything like that in Germany before.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you see any Jews in the concentration camp when you inspected it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes; there was one hut which contained Jews, but they all were under heavy sentences for economic misdemeanors and crimes, such as forging documents, and so on. We passed right through, and each one told us, without even being asked, what his sentence was and the reason for it, and not one of them told us that he was there for political reasons. The only political prisoners were the SA men.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You could not find a single prisoner there who claimed he was innocent of a crime?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No; everyone with whom we spoke related his case.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who accompanied you on that trip?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: As far as I remember, General Weber, who at that time was Chief of the General Staff. I believe also General Udet and several other gentlemen. But at the moment I do not remember who they were.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And who showed you through the concentration camp? Who guided you?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot recollect his name. It was one of the officials of the SD. I assume it was the commander of the camp himself, but I do not know his name.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And who was running the concentration camp? What organization was in charge of it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I could not say, but I presume it was one of Himmler's offices.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You have said that the march into the Rhineland was a great surprise to you?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Where were you on your leave when this occurred?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I was on winter leave in the mountains, abroad.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: In Norway?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, no.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: In which country?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I was in the Alps; I believe it was Southern Tyrol, which, at that time, was Italy.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you not hear of a meeting the minutes of which are in evidence here as Exhibit GB-160 (Document Number EC-405), concerning the Reich Defense Council meeting held on the, 26th of June 1935, some nine months before the occupation of the Rhineland?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot say whether I was present. I can no longer remember.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: There were, according to the evidence, 24 members of the Wehrmacht and five members of the Luftwaffe present, as well as 24 State and Party officials. Were you one of those present at that conference at which this discussion took place?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: May I ask again for the date?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The 26th of June 1935.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember. I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you ever learn of that meeting?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: At the moment I really cannot remember. What is supposed to have been said at that meeting?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That the preparations for the occupation of the Rhineland were to be kept secret, and the plan was made to invade the Rhineland. Did you never learn of that meeting?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember that. I do not think I was present.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: If your Honors please, the usual time for adjournment is here. I intend to take up a different subject involving some documents. It might be a convenient time to adjourn.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We will adjourn now. [The Tribunal recessed until 1400 hours.]

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I want to ask you some questions regarding your duties and activities on the Central Planning Board. You were a member of the Central Planning Board, were you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And what was the period of your membership?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: From the beginning -- I believe that was in the year 1941 or 1942 -- until the end.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Members of that Board, in addition to yourself, were the Defendant Speer?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The Defendant Funk ?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, but only later.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: When did he come on the Board?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: At the moment when a large part of the civil production was turned over to the Speer Ministry, the Ministry for Armament.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Korner? Korner was a member of the Board?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Korner? Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Who was Dr. Sauer?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Sauer was an official in the Speer Ministry, but he did not belong to the Central Planning Board.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: But he did keep some of the minutes, did he not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No; I think he did not keep them.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Sauckel frequently attended the meetings, did he not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Not frequently, but occasionally.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What were the functions of the Central Planning Board?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The distribution of raw materials to the various groups which held quotas, such as the Army, the Navy, the Air Force, and for civilian requirements for various branches such as industry, mining, industrial and private building, et cetera .

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And labor?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Pardon me, labor? We did not have to distribute that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It had nothing to do with labor? Do I understand you correctly?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: We could make suggestions, but not the distribution.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You mean by that, not the distribution amongst different industries which were competing to obtain labor?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That was a point which concerned Armaments more than the Central Planning Board.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you know that Speer turned over to the United States all of his personal papers and records, including the minutes of this Central Planning Board?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I did not know that; I hear it now.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I will ask that the minutes, volumes of minutes which constitute U.S. Document R-124, offered in evidence as French Exhibit Number RF-30, be made available for examination by the witness in the original German; I shall ask you some questions about it.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes. [Document R-124 was submitted to the witness.]

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: If, you will point out to the witness Page 1059, Line 22. This, Witness, purports to be the minutes of Conference Number 21 of the Central Planning Board, held on the 30th of October 1942 at the Reich Ministry of Armament and Munitions, and the minutes show you to have been present. Do you recall being there at that meeting?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: In that one sentence, I cannot see it, but I can well assume it. Yes. I see here in the minutes that my name is frequently mentioned.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, I call your attention -- Page 1059, Line 22 -- to the following entry and ask you if this refreshes your recollection about the functions of that Board: "Speer: The question of slackers is another point to be dealt with. Ley has ascertained that the number of people reporting sick decreased to one-fourth or one-fifth where there are factory doctors and the workers are examined by them. SS and Police could go ahead with the job and put those known as slackers into undertakings run by concentration camps. There is no other choice. Let it happen a few times, and the news will go round." Were you not concerned with the discussion of the labor situation in that conference, and does that not refresh your recollection as to the dealing with the labor question?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do recall that the question of slackers as a whole was discussed. It was rather a question of slackers, workers, people, who while not normally employed in peacetime, as a result of the total mobilization of manpower, were compelled to work during the war. Among these people, who did not belong to the ranks of the workers, I repeat that there were some slackers who upset the good spirit of the workers. It was those people we had in mind.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Those were to be sent to concentration camps, as you know?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I was told that. But no decision was arrived at. Moreover, it was not for us to send anybody to a concentration camp.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, was it not said that there was nothing to be said against the SS taking them over? You knew that the SS was running the concentration camps, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, of course.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And, therefore, you knew that turning them over to the SS and sending them to the concentration camps was a means of forcing them to produce more goods, was it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, of course, these people should be forced to do so. They were Germans who refused to do their duty to their country.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did this apply only to Germans?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: As far as I know this applied to Germans only. By slackers -- they were also called casual workers -- was meant only those people who went from place to place, who practically every week changed their job and who were reported to us mainly by the representatives of our own workers. Our own workers complained that these people availed themselves of all privileges as to food, et cetera , while they did not do anything, that they always gave up their jobs soon, and that every establishment was glad to get rid of them.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And got rid of them by sending them to the concentration camps under the SS?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: They had to be taught, and we were told that if these people had their additional -- not their basic -- rations made dependent on their output, as was the case in the concentration camps, they would very quickly learn. I do, however, remember that it was proposed to limit this treatment to 2 or 3 months, after which they would be brought back, and if they had learned their lesson they would be given full freedom again.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, did you have anything to do on, the Central Planning Board with the work of prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No; I do not think so.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, I ask that you be shown the 22d conference of the Central Planning Board minutes of the meeting held on the 2d of November 1942, Page 1042, at Line 24, which quotes you. The English translation is on Page 27. I ask you to refresh your recollection by reading this paragraph. "Milch: I think that agriculture must get its labor quota. Assuming that we had given agriculture 100,000 more workers, we would now have 100,000 more people who would be decently fed, whereas, the human material we are now receiving, particularly the prisoners of war, are not sufficiently fit for work." Did you make that statement?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember details. But I suppose I did. I do not know if I have seen these minutes; but I know that we dealt with the question that agriculture, if possible, should get its workers because the food problem was so very important, and the farms could feed their people over and above the rations which the civilian population received. This proposal to put these people on the land was quite in accordance with my views, but these were merely suggestions by the Central Planning Board. I know Sauckel was present at that meeting. We also made suggestions to the armament representatives as to how their problems could be solved.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you made recommendations to the Reich Marshal, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember having done so, I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You never did?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not know, I cannot remember.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Then you knew the Reich Marshal's wishes in reference to the utilization of prisoners of war, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That prisoners of war were also working was known to me. Especially on the land many prisoners of war were put to work.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Did you attend a meeting between the Fuehrer and Minister Speer?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: On which date?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The 5th of March 1944.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The 4th of March?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The 5th of March 1944.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: On the 5th of March, yes, I attended a meeting with the Fuehrer. At that time there was a question of creating a "fighter" staff, that is, a general effort by the entire armament industry to produce as many fighter planes as possible.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, now I will ask that you be shown Speer's memorandum of that meeting with the Fuehrer at which General Bodenschatz and Colonel Von Below were also present. Were they not? The English translation is on Page 35; the German on Page 139. I call your attention to this paragraph: "I told the Fuehrer of the Reich Marshal's wish to utilize the producing capacity of prisoners of war further by placing the Stalag under the SS, with the exception of the English and Americans. The Fuehrer approves this proposal and has asked Colonel Von Below to take the necessary steps." I ask you how the SS could increase the production of the prisoners of war; what steps you expected to be taken? Now, just answer my question. What steps did you expect the SS to take to increase the production of the prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember now. At any rate at that time we did not know what was being done by the SS -- about their methods as we now know them.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: This was in March of 1944.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, you have no knowledge of the methods by which the SS would be able to speed up production by prisoners of war. That is the way you want that to stand?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, that is not the way I want it to stand. I have to think this point over for a moment. I believe the point was whether or not prisoners of war should be made available. It was not a question of prisoners of war working for the SS, but of their being made available for work. That, I take it was the point.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Put at the disposal of the SS, you mean? Well, let us go on to the 33d Conference by the Central Planning Board, held on the 16th of February 1943, at which Speer and Sauckel among others appear to have been present. The English translation is on Page 28; the German, Pages 2276 to 2307. There was at this meeting, to summarize, considerable discussion of the labor situation, first a report from Schreiber, and then Timm gave a general account of the labor situation, and I call your attention to your contribution on Page 2298 at the top.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I have just read it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It is as follows: "Milch: We have demanded that in the anti-aircraft artillery a certain percentage of personnel should consist of Russians. Fifty thousand in all should be brought in. Thirty thousand are already employed as gunners. This is an amusing thing, that Russians must work the guns..." What was amusing about making the Russian prisoners of war work the guns?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The words "We have demanded," do not mean the Central Planning Board, but that Hitler made this demand.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: "We" means Hitler?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, the German Government. And I myself find it strange that prisoners of war should be made to shoot at planes of their allies. We did not like it because it meant that these men could no longer work for us. We were opposed to their being used in the anti-aircraft artillery.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You said: "This is an amusing thing that the Russians must work the guns." What was amusing about it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: What is meant by amusing? ... peculiar, strange, I cannot say, however, whether this word was actually used. I have not seen the minutes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, I call your attention to the rest of your contribution. ". . . 20,000 are still needed. Yesterday I received a letter from the Army High Command, stating: We cannot release any more men, we have not enough ourselves.. Thus there is no prospect for us." Whom does "for us" refer to, if not to your industry requirements?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I consider these minutes incorrect, it has never been discussed in this manner, it must be wrong. I cannot accept the minutes as they stand. To clarify this matter I may say that the proposal was to take people out of the armament industry and put them into anti-aircraft defense. We who were concerned with armament did not want to release these men and were opposed to it. That was the idea of the whole thing, and the OKH declared that they did not have enough people.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I understand the sense of this to be that you applied for certain workmen for the armament industry and that the Army High Command refused to give you the men, saying that they are already employed making guns and on other work. Now, is that the sense of that, or is it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, not quite.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, just tell me what the sense of it is.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: As far as I remember, the armament industry was to release 50,000 Russian prisoners of war to the Air Force for antiaircraft defense, and the armament industry could not spare these people.

turns.speaker: THE PRE SIDENT
turns.text: I am afraid we must adjourn due to some technical difficulty. [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Mr. Justice Jackson, it may be convenient to you to know that we are going to rise at 4:30 today.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I hope to have finished before. [Turning to the witness.] I will ask to have your attention called to Page 2297, in the English translation about Page 28, to your contribution, which reads as follows: "Milch: There is of course a front also somewhere in the East. This front will be held for a certain time. The only useful thing the Russians will find in an area evacuated by us, is people. The question is whether the people should not generally be taken back as far as 100 kilometers behind the front line. The whole civilian population goes 100 kilometers behind the front." Do you find that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I have found it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And I understood you this morning to state that it was a rule promulgated in your book that the civilian population should not be interfered with.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: From the last paragraph, according to which people were no longer to be employed on digging trenches, it appears that these people were last employed on this work. I cannot say what kind of people these were, only that they were already employed somewhere.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you knew that. You knew that they were being used for that kind of work?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: So it says here. I do not remember it any more. It has been recorded in the minutes, provided they are correct.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you knew they were being used, the civilian population was being forced to dig trenches for your troops.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Today I cannot remember any more, but at that time it was discussed according to the minutes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, I will ask, to have your attention called to the minutes of Conference Number 11 of the Central Planning Board, held on 22d of July 1942; German, Page 3062; English translation, 38. First let me call your attention to the fact that at that meeting it appears that among those present were Speer, yourself, Korner. Did Korner represent the Reich Marshal?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, for the Four Year Plan; he was the representative for the Four Year Plan.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: At all meetings of this Board, Korner represented the Reich Marshal did he not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes. He represented him as regards the Four Year Plan.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Sauckel was present, and representatives from the Iron Association, the Coal Association, and the Ministry for Armament and Munitions.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: There was considerable discussion of the labor problem, and the requirements of those industries. On Page 3062 1 call your attention to this entry: "General Field Marshal Milch undertakes to accelerate the procuring of the Russian prisoners of war from the camps." I ask you what measures you expected to take to accelerate procuring prisoners of war from the camps.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: As I was a soldier I undertook to submit this question to the OKW, which was in charge of prisoners of war.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You did not personally deal with the prisoners of war, but you undertook to obtain them from the OKW?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The government had put these prisoners of war at our disposal for work. The transfer was very slow, and as we had to deal with the OKW in this matter, I was asked and I undertook to request the OKW to speed up the transfer.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now let us turn to Conference Number 36, dated 22d of April 1943; the English translation, Page 13; German, 2125. There again I call your attention to the fact that Speer, yourself, Sauckel, and Korner were among those present. There again you discussed the labor problem, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Korner reported as follows: "On 1 April agriculture was still in need of about 600,000 workers. To cover this, labor from the East, mainly women, should be brought in. This labor must be supplied before we take other workers away from agriculture. We are now approaching a very busy season in work on the land which requires many workers," -- and considerably more, which I will not take the time to quote. I call your attention to Page 2128, your contribution to that discussion, which reads as follows: "If you do what I proposed and what has also been agreed to by Timm, no harm can be done. It should definitely be done. Moreover, I am also of the opinion that in any circumstances we have to bring in workers for coal mining. The bulk of the labor we are going to receive from the East, will be women. The women from the East are, however, accustomed to agricultural work, particularly to the kind of work which will have to be done during the next few weeks, that is, hoeing and planting of root crops, et cetera . We can use women quite well for this. Only one thing has to be kept in mind -- agriculture must get the women before the men are taken away. It would be wrong to take men away and to leave the farmers without labor 'for 4 to 6 weeks. If the women come after that, it will be too late." I ask you how many women were transported to agriculture as a result of this conference?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: As a result of this conference none at all, as only suggestions were put forward by us for an arrangement between industry and agriculture to procure the necessary labor for the former. Without the necessary labor in the coal-mining industry the war could not be carried on. Therefore labor had to be found, and in this respect a suggestion was made for an exchange, namely, to replace men engaged in agriculture by women, who, of course, could not be put to work in the mines.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: To whom did you make these suggestions? You say they were not decisions but just suggestions.

turns.speaker: MMCH
turns.text: No. The suggestions were made to representatives of the Ministry of Labor or to the Office for the Allocation of Labor. I see Timm is mentioned. He was one of the higher officials in this ministry.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And Sauckel?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not know whether Sauckel attended that conference. I see only Timm's name.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It appears from the minutes that he was there; but whether he was or not, you made suggestions to Sauckel as to the needs for labor, did you not, and called upon him to supply them?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes; it was necessary to get workers for coal mining. New workers could not be found, thus there was no alternative but to make an exchange.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: We understand you. You will save a great deal of our time if you will just answer the questions. Now I call your attention to Conference Number 54 of the Central Planning Board, held on 1 March 1944; English translation Page 1, German Page 1762. At this conference I remind you that it appears that Sauckel, Milch, Schreiber, and Korner were among those present. It was held at the Air Ministry and you discussed the desirability of draining off young men from France so that they would not be available to act as partisans in case there was an invasion by the Allies of French territory. Do you recall such a meeting?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember details. In the course of other interrogations here in Nuremberg and in England I already stated that it is impossible to remember in detail all these matters, which were heaped upon us, especially as my memory has suffered through heavy blows on the head received at the time of my capture.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: It will help you if you will refer to Page 1799, opposite the name "Milch" and read the entry, as follows: "Milch: If landings take place in France and more or less succeed, we will have in France a partisan uprising, such as we never had in the Balkans or in the East, not because the people are particularly able to carry it through, but because we allow them to do so by failing to deal with them in the right manner. Four entire age groups have grown up in France, men between 18 and 23, that is, of an age when young people, for patriotic reasons or because they have been stirred up, are prepared to do anything to satisfy personal hatred -- and it is only natural that they do hate us. These young men should have been registered according to age groups and brought to us, as they constitute the greatest danger in the event of a landing. "I am firmly convinced, and have said so several times, that if and when the invasion starts, acts of sabotage to railways, works, and supply bases will be a daily occurrence. The Wehrmacht, however, will then no longer be able to deal with this internal situation, as it will have to fight at the front and will have in its rear a very dangerous enemy who will threaten supplies, et cetera . If severe executive measures had been taken, all would have been as quiet as the grave behind the front at a time when things were about to happen. I have drawn attention to this several times, but I am afraid nothing is being done. When we have to start shooting these people, it will already be too late. We shall no longer have the men to polish off the partisans." You then go on to state that you think the Army should handle the executive action required in rounding up these people. Does that refresh your recollection?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, that was roughly what I meant to say, but I cannot say whether I used these very words. In this life and death struggle of our country we had to make sure that we were not suddenly stabbed in the back by a secret army, as unfortunately happened later on.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And you proposed to eliminate the population behind the lines insofar as they might constitute a menace to your operations in this invasion?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, it was proposed to send these people at the right time to work in Germany, as had been promised by the French Government. That was my view. It was necessary that these people should come to work in Germany, as the French Government had promised in its agreement with the German Government, instead of allowing these people to join the Maquis and commit sabotage, which would necessitate shootings as a countermeasure.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You did not confine your use of forced labor to your enemies; it was also applied against your own allies, was it not? For example turn to Page 1814, and did you not contribute to this discussion? "Milch: Would not the S-factories" -- that is, protected factories -- "be better protected if we handle the whole problem of feeding the Italians and tell them: 'You will get your food only if you work in S-factories or come to Germany.'"

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That was after a part of Italy had broken away, and it applied to Italian soldiers who had declared themselves against Mussolini. These people remained behind the front, did not want to work, and committed sabotage against the German Armed Forces. Thus it was proposed to say to these people, "You will have your food and everything else provided, but you will have to work somewhere, either in Italy in the iron ore mines, or in Germany."

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I think you said in your direct examination, or perhaps earlier in your cross-examination, that you did not know about any forced labor from occupied territory, you had no knowledge of that. Is that still your statement?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I did not quite understand that. Forced labor?

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Forced labor, yes.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You did not know about it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: These people were prisoners of war, Italians, who were at our disposal for work according to an agreement with the Italian Government which we had recognized. Mussolini had expressly put these men at our disposal for this purpose.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Excuse me for interrupting you, but let us not bother with Mussolini here. I ask you whether you still stand by the statement you made earlier, as I recall it, that you did not know of any forced labor brought in from the occupied countries to Germany. Is that your statement, or is it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Insofar as they were free workers and free people, I still maintain this. My point is that these were people who had been placed at our disposal, and, Mr. Justice, as far as we are concerned, at the time this was said there was still an Italian Government, though this fact is forgotten today; but at that time it still existed.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I ask that your attention be brought to Page 1827 of the minutes of this meeting at which you were present, and where the discussion you just admitted took place; and I call your attention to the line opposite the name "Sauckel," from which it appears that Sauckel then reported: "Out of the 5 million foreign workers who arrived in Germany, not even 200,000 came voluntarily."

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I cannot remember that at all.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You do not have any recollection of that? All right.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I have no recollection of that.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, we will go on then to Conference Number 23 of the Central Planning Board, held the 3rd of November 1942. It is the English translation, Page 27. The German text is on Page 1024, in which it appears that you were present at and participated in the discussion, and I can your attention to Page 1024, Line 10, to these entries of the stenographic minutes: "Speer: Well, under the pretext of industry we could deceive the French into believing that we would release all prisoners of war who are rollers and smelters if they give us the names. "Rohland: We have installed our own office in Paris. I see, you mean the French should give the names of the smelters who are prisoners of war in Germany? "Milch: I would simply say, you get two men in exchange for one. "Speer: The French firms know exactly which prisoners of war are smelters. Unofficially, you should create the impression that they would be released. They give us the names and then we get them out. Have a try. "Rohland: That is an idea." Now, your contribution was to want two men in place of one; is that right?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes; that is to say, two people from another trade for one of these particular skilled workers. In what straits we were, you can see from...

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That was your entire objective?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The entire purpose was to get these people and to give them others in exchange.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, let us take up Conference Number 53 of the Planning Board, held the 16th of February 1944; English translation, Page 26, and the German from Page 1851 on. You will find yourself included among those who were present and it was at the Reich Air Ministry that it was held. I first call your attention to the entry on Page 1863, the words opposite "Milch": "The armament industry employs foreign workers in, large numbers; according to the latest figures, 40%. The latest allocations from the Plenipotentiary General for the Allocation of Labor are mostly foreigners and we had to give up manyGerman workers in the recruitment drive. Particularly the aircraft industry, which is a young industry, employs a great many young men who should be called up. This will, however, be very difficult, as those working for experimental stations cannot be touched. In mass production, the foreign workers preponderate and in some instances represent 95 percent and even more; 88 percent of the workers engaged in the production of our newest engines are Russian prisoners of war and the 12 percent are German men and women. On the Ju-52, which are now regarded as transport planes only, and the monthly production of which is from 50 to 60 machines, only six to eight German workers are engaged; the rest are Ukrainian women who have lowered the record of production of skilled workers." Do you recall that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I can remember that distinctly.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And on Page 1873, you come forward with this suggestion: "Milch: The list of slackers should be handed to Himmler. He will make them work all right. This is of a great general educational importance, and has also a deterrent effect on others who would also like to shirk."

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, this applies again to the slackers in agriculture as I mentioned this morning.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Among foreign workers, was it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No; these were Englishmen, the slackers.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Englishmen are foreigners in Germany, are they not? I do not know what you mean, they were not foreigners. They were Englishmen.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Englishmen never worked for us. So they cannot have been Englishmen.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What were they? You say they were all German.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: What we understood as slackers were those people who were compelled to work during the war, Germans who normally were not regular workers, but were forcibly made to work during the war.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: We will get to that in a minute. First, I want to ask you how Himmler was going to make them work. What did Himmler do, what methods did Himmler use? Why were you making proposals to Himmler in this matter?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Because Himmler at a meeting had stated that as regards supplementary rations -- the worker in Germany had the same basic rations as the rest of the population, and apart from this he received quite considerable additions which in the case of those doing the heaviest work were several times the normal basic rations. The general routine was that these rations were issued by food offices, irrespective of where and how the individual was working. The suggestion was made by Himmler that these additions should be made dependent upon the output of the workers. This was possible in the case of those workers who came from concentration camps, et cetera , and were under Himmler. This procedure could not be applied to free workers; hence the proposal to bring to reason those who sabotaged work in their own country, by issuing additional I rations, as laid down for their type of work, only in proportion to their output.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You know the difference between labor camps and concentration camps, do you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, of course.
