turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And these people who were doing work in these industries were kept mainly in the work camps, were they not, in which their rations were controlled without Himmler's hands being in it at all?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No; the German workers were not kept in labor camps but they lived at home and, therefore, received their additional rations from the local food offices. I want to stress again that it was the German workers themselves who asked that measures be taken -- the factory foremen, who were infuriated to see that people who did not do anything, who let their country down in times of stress, received more rations than ordinary civilians.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: You still say that all you are talking about were German and never foreign workers. Now, be clear about that.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: By slackers I meant German workers; in my opinion, only these were in question.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: I ask that your attention be called to Page 1913: This is your contribution at that point: "Milch: It is therefore quite impossible to utilize every foreigner fully unless we make them do piecework and are in a position to take measures against foreigners who are not doing their bit." Do you find that entry?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: And then you proceed to complain that: "If a foreman lays his hands on a prisoner of war and boxes his ears, there is at once a terrible row; the man is put in prison, and so on. There are many officials in Germany who consider it their first duty to stand up for other men's human rights instead of looking after war production. I, too, am for human rights, but if a Frenchman says, 'You fellows will be hanged and the works manager will be the first to have his head cut off and then if the boss says, 'I'll give him one for that,' then he is in for it. Nobody sides with the manager, but only with the 'poor devil' who said that to him." Did you report that to the meeting?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That may well be the case.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: What did you suggest?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I can remember cases where foreign workers threatened and even assaulted their German foreman, and when he defended himself action was taken against him. I did not think it right.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Now, you provided your own remedy, did you not? In the next line you say: "I told my engineers, 'If you do not hit a man like this, then I shall punish you. The more you do in this respect, the more I shall think of you; I shall see to it that nothing happens to you.' This has not yet gone round. I cannot talk to every works manager individually. But I should like to see some one try to stop me, as I can deal with anyone who tries it." Do you find that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember the exact words but I stick to the point that it was an impossible situation for a prisoner or foreign worker to be able to say to his German foreman, "We will cut your throat," and the foreman...

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, do you mean to say that if a prisoner of war attempted or threatened to cut his employer's throat, that German officers would stand up for him as against the employer? You do not mean that, do you? [There was no response.]

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Well, we will go on: "If the small works manager" -- I am still quoting from you -- "does that, he is put into a concentration camp..." Do you find that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I see it here.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: ". . and runs the risk of having his prisoners of war taken from him." Now, I am still quoting you and I want you to find the entry. "In one case, two Russian officers took off with an airplane but crashed. I ordered that these two men be hanged at once. They were hanged or shot yesterday. I left that to the SS. I wanted them to be hanged in the factory for the others to see." Do you find that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I have found it, and I can only say I have never had anybody hanged nor have I even given such an order. I could not possibly have said such a thing. I had nothing to do with this question. Neither do I know of any instance where two Russian officers tried to escape by plane.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Is there anything else you would like to say with reference to that entry?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. I have nothing to say. I do not know anything about it and I also do not believe I ever said it.

turns.speaker: MR. JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: That is all that I have at the present time.

turns.speaker: MR. G. D. ROBERTS (Leading Counsel for the United Kingdom)
turns.text: Witness, I have some questions on behalf of the British Delegation. My first point is this: You said on Friday that, beginning in 1935, an air force was built up in Germany for defensive purposes. Do you remember that?

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: And do you say that it remained on a defensive basis up to December 1939?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You do. I want you to listen to three pieces of evidence -- speeches made by your chief, the Defendant Goering. I am quoting from the shorthand notes of the 8th of January, in the afternoon, on Page 2306. In May 1935, Goering said: "I intend to create a Luftwaffe which, if the hour should strike, will burst upon the foe like an avenging host. The enemy must feel that he has lost even before he has started fighting." Does that sound like a defensive air force?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, that does not sound like it; but one has to distinguish between words and deeds.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I shall come to the deeds in a moment. [Laughter.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: If there is any more of this laughter, the Court will have to be cleared.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: On the 8th of July 1938 Goering, addressing a number of German aircraft manufacturers, said: "War with Czechoslovakia is imminent; the German Air Force is already superior to the English Air Force. If Germany wins the war, she will be the greatest power in the world; she will dominate the world markets, and Germany will be a rich nation. To attain this goal risks must be taken." Does that sound like a defensive German Air Force? Does it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, that certainly does not sound like it. I should like to be allowed to say something to that, when you have finished.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Please limit yourself, if you can, in the interest of time, to answering my question, which is very short. Now may I read you one further piece of evidence; the speech made by Goering on 14 October 1938, that is less than a month after the Munich Pact. "Hitler has ordered me to organize a gigantic armament program, which would make all previous achievements appear insignificant. I have been ordered to build as rapidly as possible an air force five times as large as the present one." Does that sound like an air force for defensive purposes?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: This air force would have taken many years to build.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I suggest to you that your evidence on that point was grossly incorrect. I now want to come to my second point. You were, present at the conference of chiefs of the services in the Chancellery on 23 May 1939?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: What was the date please?

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I would like you to see the document, which is L-79. You did see it on Friday, I think.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: On 23 May, was it not?

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Yes, that is right. I just want to remind you who else was present. There were the Fuehrer, Goering, Raeder, Von Brauchitsch, Keitel , yourself, Halder, General Bodenschatz, Warlimont -- was Warlimont the deputy for Jodl?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot say for whom he was there.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Very well -- and others; I will not mention the names. Now, Witness, those were leaders of the German Armed Forces?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: May I say, as far as I can remember Field Marshal Goering was not present. I cannot remember.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: He is down there as being present. You think he was not there?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes. I cannot remember, but, to my recollection I was sent there at the last moment to represent him.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, then, apart from Goering, if he was not there, those were mostly the leaders of the German forces, is that right? MILCH, Yes. It was the Commander-in-Chief of the Army and the Commander-in-Chief of the Navy, and the OKW, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Would you describe them, from your knowledge of them, as men of honor?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Is it one of the qualities of a man of honor that he keeps his word?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You knew, of course, did you not, that Germany had pledged her word to respect the neutrality of Belgium, of the Netherlands, and Luxembourg?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I suppose so, but I did not know the various agreements.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did you not know that less than a month before that meeting, namely on the 28th of April, Hitler in the Reichstag gave an assurance of his respect for the neutrality of a large number of countries, European countries, including the three I have mentioned? Did you not know that as a matter of history?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I suppose so, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: We have seen the film, you know, in this Court, of that very occurrence with the Defendant Goering presiding as President of the Reichstag while that assurance was given.

turns.speaker: MIILCH
turns.text: I have not seen the film. I do not know the film.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Yes. It is a German newsreel. Do you remember that at that conference Hitler said these words, which are well known to the Tribunal: "The Dutch and Belgian air bases must be occupied by the Armed Forces. Declarations of neutrality must be ignored.... An effort must be made to deal the enemy a heavy or decisive final blow right at the start. Considerations of right or wrong, or treaties, do not enter into the matter." Do you remember those words being said?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember exactly what the words were. I know that it was a question of the Polish Corridor and Danzig, that in this connection Hitler explained what complications might follow in the West, and what he intended to do about it; but what he said in detail I can no longer remember.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Was any protest made by any of these honorable men at the breach of Germany's pledged word?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: During this meeting it was impossible for anyone present to speak at all. Hitler addressed us from his desk, and after the speech he left the room. A discussion did not take place; he did not allow it.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You say it is impossible for an honorable man to protect his honor, Witness?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember Hitler's actual words shown here.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Can you give the Tribunal your opinion of it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: At this meeting I did not have the impression that Hitler said anything contrary to the obligations entered into. That I cannot remember.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Are you now saying that those minutes are wrong?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I cannot say that either. I can only say I have no recollection of the exact words used. Whether the minutes are completely correct I do not know either. As far as I know they were recorded subsequently by one of the adjutants present.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Because we know that is exactly what Germany did 12 months after, when she broke her pledged word to Belgium, to the Netherlands, and Luxembourg, and brought misery and death to millions. You know that now, do you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That I know, yes; but as soldiers we had nothing to do with the political side. We were not asked about that.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Do you call the honoring of...

turns.speaker: DR. RUDOLPH DIX (Counsel for the Defendant Schacht )
turns.text: I do not speak now for the Defendant Schacht, but for the entire Defense. I ask the Tribunal that the witness be questioned about facts, and not about his opinion as to moral standards.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: He is being asked about facts.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You have just said that you know now -- we know, that 12 months later Germany did violate the neutrality of Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: But we do not know what the reasons were for this, and what other obligations these countries might have entered into. It was not a job of the soldiers to judge this.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Was it not a job of the soldier to object if he was asked to break his country's word?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I fully agree with you, if a soldier breaks his word in matters which are his province and where he has a say as a soldier. As regards matters quite outside his province, which he cannot judge and about which he knows nothing, he cannot be made responsible and called to account.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You can only speak for your own knowledge. Are you saying that you did not know that your country was pledged to observe the neutrality of these three small countries?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That I have read in the Reichstag speech. But I did not know how the other side had reacted to that promise. It was not known to me, and it could easily be that the other side did not at all want this protection, or this promise, or this guarantee. The soldier could not judge this at all; only the political authorities could know this.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, we perhaps will have to ask that of the soldiers in the High Command, who are now in the dock, when they get in the witness box. But I put it to you it must have been common knowledge in Germany that Hitler was giving guarantees and assurances to all these smaller countries?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Hitler proposed and offered many things. He offered limitations of armaments for all countries; he offered not to use bombers; but in these cases also his proposals were not accepted. Therefore the political authorities alone could know what they should and could demand from their soldiers. The only duty of a soldier is to obey.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Will you please answer my question. That was not an answer at all to my question. We know the facts now, Witness, from the documents, from your own German documents. I want to test your knowledge and your ideas of honor. Did you not think it grossly dishonorable to give a pledge on 28 April, and to make secret resolution to break it on 23 May?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: You are right if the situation had not changed in any way, and that I cannot judge.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You must have your own code of honor, even though you are in the service. You know, of course, that the neutrality of Norway was violated?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, according to our knowledge and in our opinion it was violated twice.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Do you know that on the 12th and 13th of March 1940 Jodl was putting in his diary, "The Fuehrer is still looking for a pretext" to give out to the world for an invasion of Norway? Do you know that?

turns.speaker: MIELCH
turns.text: I do not know this diary and this entry.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You took an active part in the invasion of Norway, did you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: A few days after the invasion started I was in command of the air force up there for a short time.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You had actually a command in Norway?

turns.speaker: MIELCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. JAHRREISS
turns.text: I think it necessary to clear up a point which apparently concerns a misunderstanding by the interpreter. I have just heard that a diary entry by the Defendant Jodl has been wrongly translated back into German. The German text says "nach einer Begrundung," that is "for a justification." I also believe the word "justification" is in the English translation. It should not have been interpreted as "Ausrede," that would be "pretexte" in French and that is something quite different.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Whatever it reads in the translation, Witness, would you agree that according to the entry in the diary, the Fuehrer was still looking for it, whether it was a reason or an excuse? Now I want to ask you only one more question on this side of he case. You know that Belgrade was bombed in, I think, April 1941?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I heard about that from the Army report at the time.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Without any declaration of war, or any warning to the civilian population at all, you heard that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That I do not know, no.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did you not discuss it with Goering?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: The attack on Belgrade? No; I cannot remember.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did not even he express regret, shall we say, regarding the large-scale bombing of a large capital without even one hour warning to the civilian population?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not know. I cannot remember any such conversation.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: That is murder, is it not? [There was no response.]

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Perhaps you would rather not answer that question?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot answer "yes" or "no," because I know nothing of the circumstances of the attack. I do not know whether war had been declared; I do not know whether a warning had been given. Neither do I know whether Belgrade was a fortress, nor which targets were attacked in Belgrade. I know of so many bombing attacks about which the same questions could be asked in the same manner.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I asked the question, Witness, because we had the use of the document in front of us, and knew that it was Hitler's order that Belgrade was to be suddenly destroyed by waves of bombers, without any ultimatum, or any diplomatic arguments, or negotiations at all. Would I put that question if I had not known of the document? Let me turn to something else.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: May I say I have heard of this document only today because you quoted it.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I want to put to you now an incident with regard to the Camp Stalag Luft III at Sagan. Do you know about what I am talking?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I know about that now.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Do you know that on 24 and 25 March 1944 about 80 air force officers, British and Dominion, with some others, escaped from the Stalag Luft III Camp?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I know about this from the British interrogation camp in which I was kept, where the whole case was posted up on the wall.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: We will come to that in a moment. Do you know that of those 80, 50 were shot?

turns.speaker: MIELCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: In various parts of Germany and the occupied countries from Danzig to Saarbrucken; you have heard of that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I heard that about 50 were shot, but did not know where.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Have you heard that quite unusually the bodies were never seen again, but that urns said to contain their ashes were brought back to the camp; you heard of that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I heard of it in the camp, where I was kept, from Mr. Anthony Eden's speech in the House of Commons.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You heard that although these officers were reported by your Government as having been shot while offering resistance or trying to escape, yet not one was wounded, and all 50 were shot dead.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: At first I heard only the official report in Germany, that these officers had been shot while resisting or trying to escape. We did not believe this version and there was a lot of discussion about this without precise knowledge. We were afraid that these men might have been murdered.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You were afraid that murder had been committed. It does appear likely, does it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: We got that impression, as the various details we heard could not be pieced together.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: It is quite clear that if that was murder, the order for that murder would have to come from a high level, is it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Certainly. I heard further details about this from the Inspector General for Prisoners of War, General Westhoff, while both of us were in captivity in England.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Now, I want to ask you, first of all, about the Prisoner-of-War Organization. Was the Prisoner-of-War Organization a department of the OKW?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: In my opinion, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Which was called KGW, Kriegsgefangenenwesen?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot say anything about its organization, because I do not know. I only knew that there was a chief of the Kriegsgefangenenwesen with the OKW.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: And was the chief of the Kriegsgefangenenwesen at that time Major General Von Graevenitz?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Von Graevenitz, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: This was an air force camp? Stalag Luft III was an air force camp?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes. So it was called, but I understand that all prisoners were under the OKW. That is what I thought. I cannot, however, state this definitely because I did not know much about that organization.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Was the directorate for supervising the air force camps, or the inspectorate, rather, called Inspectorate Number 17?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: There was an inspectorate, which as its name indicated had to deal with supervision. What it had to do and what were its tasks, I cannot say. Whether it was just for interrogation, I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Was the head of that Major General Grosch?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot say, it is possible, I know the name but not whether he held that post.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: And the second in command, Colonel Waelde?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Not known to me.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You were Number 2 in the Air Force at the Air Ministry in March 1944, were you not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: There were several Number 2 people at that time. I held the same rank as the chief of the general staff, the chief of the personnel office, and the chief of technical armament, who were independent of me and on the same level. As to seniority, I ranked as second officer in the Air Force.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Was there a conference in Berlin on the morning of Saturday, the 25th of March, about this escape?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did not Goering speak to you about that conference?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I have no recollection.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did Goering never tell you that them was a conference between Hitler, Himmler, himself, and Keitel on that Saturday morning?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. I do not know anything about that. I do not remember.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: At which the order for the murder of these recaptured prisoners of war was given?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember. According to what I heard later, the circumstances were entirely different. I had information about this from the previously mentioned General Westhoff and also from General Bodenschatz.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: General Westhoff we are going to see here as a witness. He has made a statement about the matter saying ...

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I beg your pardon. I could not hear you just now. The German is coming through very faintly. I can hear you, but not the German transmission.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: General Westhoff...

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: ... has made a statement...

turns.speaker: MIILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: ... and we are going to see him as a witness.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: So perhaps I had better not put his statement to you, because he is going to give evidence. Perhaps that would be fairer from the point of view of the Defense. But are you suggesting that action against these officers, if they were murdered -- to use your words -- having escaped from an air force camp, that action could have been taken without the knowledge of Goering?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I consider it quite possible in view of the great confusion existing in the highest circles at that time.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: High confusion in March 1944?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: All through there was terrible confusion.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: But it is quite clear ...

turns.speaker: MIILCH
turns.text: Hitler interfered in all matters, and himself gave orders over the heads of the chiefs of the Wehrmacht.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: But did you never discuss this matter with Goering at all?

turns.speaker: MIELCH
turns.text: No. I cannot remember ever speaking to Goering about this question.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Do you not think this is a matter which reflects shame on the Armed Forces of Germany?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes; that is a great shame.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Yet Goering never spoke to you about it at all? Did you ever speak to Keitel?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I could not say. During that time I hardly ever saw Goering.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did you ever speak to Keitel about it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, never. I saw even less of Keitel than of Goering.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Was there not a General Foster or Foerster at the Air Ministry?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, there was.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: General Foerster?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Was he director of operations?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No. He was chief of the Luftwehr. As such he had to deal with replacements of personnel and he worked with the departments concerned, with the General Staff, and also the Reich Marshal. During the war he was also in charge of civil aviation, and in that capacity he worked together with me, but during the war it was a very small job ...

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I was going to ask you, did he ever mention this shooting to you?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I have been asked that before, but try as I may I cannot remember. It is possible that in the course of conversation he may have told me that officers had been shot, but whether he did so, and in what way, under what circumstances, I cannot recollect. I did not receive an official report from him; I had no right to ask for one either.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: If Foerster told you, did you ever report it to Goering?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot remember a conversation with Foerster about it: I do not think I spoke to him. He did not give me a report either, which I should have had to pass on to Goering. Such a report would have been given by him to Goering direct, through quite different channels and much quicker.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did you take any steps to prevent this shooting from being carried out?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: When I first heard about it it was not clear to me what had actually happened. But even if it had been clear, it was evident from what Westhoff told me that it would unfortunately have been too late.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Why too late?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Because Westhoff was the first officer to have knowledge of it. When he was informed he was told that the order had already been carried out. I may say that General Westhoff made this statement and will confirm it.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Very well, you never went to Goering at all in the matter, as you say.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not know anything about it.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Now I am going to deal further with three short points. With regard to the use of labor for the armament industry, Mr. Justice Jackson has asked you questions on that. Was labor from concentration camps used?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Would you just look at Document Number 1584-PS: That is shorthand note 1357, 12 December, in the afternoon. Is that a teletype from Goering to Himmler, dated 14 February 1944? There are various code numbers; then, to ReichsFuehrer SS -- that was Himmler, Reichsminister Himmler. Who actually sent that teletype? It is signed by Goering, but he would not be dealing with questions of labor, would he?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I could not say, I could not say from whom it originated.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: That was a subject with which you dealt, was it not, the provision of labor for air armament?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Only while I had to do with air armament did I send demands for labor to the respective offices. But this telegram did not come from my office.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: If it did not come from your office, whose office did it come from?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: It deals with various matters, there is first the question of another squadron.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Please answer the question, whose office did it come from?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot say that off-hand.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Very well.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Second sentence: "At the same time I request that a substantial number of concentration camp prisoners be put at my disposal for air armament, as this kind of labor has proved to be very useful." You had frequently used concentration camp labor, had you?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Latterly, yes. May I ask, is the teletype dated the 15th and what is the month?

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Yes, I told you, Witness, 14 February 1944. It is on the top.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I could not read it here.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: No, I quite understand. And did Himmler respond by providing you with 90,000 further concentration camp prisoners? I refer to Document 1584-PS, Number 3, dated 9 March 1944. It is to the "Most Honored Reich Marshal" from Heinrich Himmler. It says: "At present apprwdmately 36,000 prisoners are employed for the Air Force. It is proposed to bring the number up to 90,000." Then he refers in the last paragraph: "The transfer of aircraft manufacturing plants underground requires a further 100,000 prisoners." Now, those were concentration camp internees, Witness?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes; I see that from the letter.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You said you were almost ignorant of the conditions in concentration camps?

turns.speaker: MIFLCH
turns.text: No; I do not know anything about that.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You have not seen the films taken when the camps were captured?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: The grim contrast -- just wait a moment -- the grim contrast between the plump and well-fed guards and civilians and the skeletons of the internees?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I have not seen the film, but I saw photographs when I was in England.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did you close your eyes deliberately to what was going on in Germany?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, it was not possible for us to see it.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: You, in your position, could not know what was going on? MrLCH: It was absolutely impossible.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Now then, I just want to deal very shortly with a matter upon which Mr. Justice Jackson touched, but he did not read the letter. That is the question of the experiments for the purpose of Air Force research. I am amdous to refer to as few documents as possible, but I can give the reference. Do you know that on 15 May 1941, and the reference is shorthand note 1848, Document Number 1602-PS, that Dr. Rascher wrote to Himmler?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I did not know him. I think I mentioned that during my interrogation.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: He had very dangerous experiments to make for which no human being would volunteer. Monkeys were not suitable, so he asked for human subjects which Himmler at once provided -- said he would be glad to provide human subjects for the experiment. Now, that was in 1941. Did you know that was taking place?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I did not know anything about that.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Now, Rascher was...

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I did not know Rascher personally.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: He was a doctor on the staff of the Air Force.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: But, Mr. Roberts, this is not a letter to this witness, is it?

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: My Lord, I am leading up to it. The next letter is a letter signed by this witness. That was preliminary. Perhaps I had better come to the letter which he signed now; I am much obliged. I want to put to you now Document Number 343-PS, and I also want to put to you, if the officer in charge of the documents would be so good, I want to put to you Document Number 607-PS.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Mr. Roberts, he has already been crossexamined upon this letter, has he not?

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I did not think the letter was read or was dealt with sufficiently. I believe Your Lordship thinks it was.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The letter was put to him. I do not know whether it was actually read.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I shall be guided by the Court entirely. I know the matter was touched upon. I felt perhaps the letter should be read but I may be quite wrong.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I am told it was not read but the two letters were put to him.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: I agree. If Your Lordship would be good enough to bear with me for a very few minutes I can perhaps deal with the matters I think should be dealt with. [Turning to the witness.] You will see that on the 20th of May 1942 -- this is your letter to "Wolffy," is it not, that is ObergruppenFuehrer Wolff, and that is signed by you is it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I signed it. That is the letter which, as I said this morning was submitted to me by the Medical Inspection department and from which it appears that we wanted to dissociate ourselves from the whole business as politely as possible.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: The point of the letter is, if I may summarize it, that you say: "In reference to your telegram of 12 May our Medical Inspection department..."

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Mr. Roberts, if I remember right, when these letters were put to the witness he said he had not read them; that he signed them without reading them.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, My Lord, perhaps I had better leave the matter if Your Lordship thinks I am going over ground which has been trodden too often. [Turning to the witness.] Are you asking this Tribunal to believe that you signed these two letters to Wolff, who was liaison officer, was he not, between -- who was Wolff?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, Wolff was not liaison officer, he was Himmler's adjutant. He sent a telegram to us, apparently for the attention of the Medical Inspection department. The Medical Inspection department replied via my office because for some reason or other it did not appear expedient to reply direct. I stated in my interrogations that these letters, though signed by me, were not dictated in my office, but that for this reply from the Medical Inspection department my stationery was used as was customary. I had nothing to do either with our high altitude experiments or with the Medical Inspection department, nor was I in any way connected with experiments by the SS.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did you know that these pressure chamber experiments were being carried out with human bodies, human souls, provided by Dachau?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: On whom they were made appears from the letter submitted to me by the Medical Inspection department. In the Air Force we made many experiments with our own medical officers who volunteered for it; and as we did it with our own people we considered it to be our own affair. We, therefore, did not want any experiments by the SS; we were not interested in them. We had for a very long time experimented with our own people. We did not need the SS, who interfered in a matter which did not concern them; and we could never understand why the SS meddled with this matter.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did not Himmler write you a letter -- the reference is shorthand note 1852 -- in November 1942, that is Document Number 1617-PS, in which he says: "Dear Milch: ... both high pressure and cold water experiments have been carried out...." and that he, Himmler, provided asocial persons and criminals from concentration camps? Do you remember that letter?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: This letter was shown to me but I cannot remember this letter either. I do not know why Himmler wrote to me at all. These letters were always passed on direct by my office, without my seeing them, to the respective offices of the Medical Inspection department and replied to via my office. I was not in a position to do anything in this respect because I did not know what it was all about, nor had I any idea of the medical aspect.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: If you say you know nothing about letters which you signed I cannot carry the matter any further. Now I want to deal with the last point.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: During the course of the day I had to sign several hundred letters and I could not know what they dealt with in detail. In this particular case it was a question for a specialist and I merely signed in order to relieve the Medical Inspector of responsibility who, for the reason mentioned this morning, did not want to sign himself.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Very well, I am leaving that point. Now then, the last point. You said on Friday that a German general has been executed for looting jewelry. Where did the looting take place?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot say that. I seem to recollect that it was in Belgrade. The name of the general is General Wafer, this I still remember.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: It was jewelry looted from Belgrade?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That I cannot say. I know only what I said on Friday.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: So the German authorities regarded the death penalty as a suitable one for looting; apparently that is right.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I could not hear the question.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, perhaps it was a comment. I will ask you the next question. What was the value of the jewelry which was looted?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I can say only that I do not know how it was stolen, or what was stolen, or how valuable it was; but only that it was said to be jewelry which he had appropriated and that he was sentenced to death.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did Goering ever speak to you about his art collection he was getting from occupied countries?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not know anything about that.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: May I read you a piece of evidence, shorthand note 2317, and it is an order of Goering signed on the 5th of November 1940. "Goering to the Chief of the Military Administration in Paris and to the Einsatzstab Rosenberg: "To dispose of the art objects brought to the Louvre in the following order of priority: "First, those art objects. . ."

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Mr. Roberts, he has never seen this document and he says he knows nothing about it.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: If your Lordship please, if you do not think I should put it to him ... [Turning to the witness.] You say Goering never discussed with you his art collection?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: Did you not know that valuable art objects, according to an inventory over 21,000 objects, were taken from the western occupied countries? '

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No; that is not known to me.

turns.speaker: MR. ROBERTS
turns.text: What ought the general who looted the jewelry, perhaps from Belgrade, to have done with it? Given it to the Fuehrer, or given it to Goering?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I ask to be excused from answering this question.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Will you please tell me when you heard of Hitler's plan to go to war with the Soviet Union? In January 1941?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: As I said on Friday, I heard in January from Reich Marshal Goering that Hitler had told him he expected there would be an attack on Russia. Then for several months I heard nothing more about the whole thing, until by chance I found out from a subordinate that war with Russia was imminent and preparations for the clothing of the troops were being made.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Did you know about Case Barbarossa?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I had heard the name, and I heard the plan expounded at a Fuehrer conference with the commanders of the various army groups and armies 1 or 2 days before the attack.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: And when did this take place -- 1, 2 days before the invasion?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I will let you know the exact date in a minute.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Please do.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: On 14 June. That is about eight days before the attack which took place on the 22d.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: And before that, you had neither heard of, nor seen this plan?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I say that I had probably heard the name Barbarossa before.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: And how long before?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That I cannot say, because during the months of January, February, March, and also in April I was outside Germany and I did not return until May. I was in Africa, Greece, Yugoslavia, and the West,

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I am interested in the period when you were in the High Command of the German Air Force. Were you in Germany in December and January?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: In December 1940.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: So?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Only part of December as during that month I was in France and also in Italy. GEN. RUDENKO; And where were you in January 1941?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I was in the West, and as far as I remember not one day in Germany.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: But you just told us that in January 1941 you had a talk with Goering about the plan of war against the Soviet Union.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, I...

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: In January 1941?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, on 13 January, but I cannot say now whether I spoke to Goering in France, or whether it was over the telephone, or whether I was in Germany for a day or two. That I cannot say, I did not make a note of it.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Excuse me; what has a telephone conversation to do with an attack on the Soviet Union?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Not an attack on Russia, but an attack by Russia on Germany was mentioned at that time, and we had..

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: You mean to say you discussed over the telephone the question of an attack by the Soviet Union on Germany?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: No, I have not stated anything like that, but I said I do not know whether I received the information on a special line which could not be tapped, or whether the Reich Marshal told me about it in France, or whether on that particular day I was in Germany.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: And when did you discuss this question with Goering, and when did Goering express his apprehension as to this war against the Soviet Union?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That was on 22 May.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: The 22nd of May 1941?

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: And where was this question discussed?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: In Veldenstein near Nuremberg.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Did you discuss this question with Goering alone, or was anybody else present at this conversation?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: At that time only with Goering. We were alone.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: And you assert that Goering did not wish to go to war with Russia?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That was my impression.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: So. And why did Goering not want this war against the Soviet Union? This was a defensive war, was it not?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Goering was opposed to such a war, because he wanted, all of us did...

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: He was opposed also to a defensive war?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: He personally was against any war.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: That is strange. Maybe you will be able to give me precise reasons why Goering did not wish war against the Soviet Union.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Because a war on two fronts, especially a war against Russia, as I saw it, meant losing the war; and I believe that many fighting men and others thought as I did.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: So you too were opposed to a war against the Soviet Union?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes, most definitely so.

turns.speaker: GEN.RUDENKO
turns.text: Strange. Your statements are not very consistent. On the one hand, you say that the Soviet Union was going to attack Germany, and on the other hand that German officers did not want a war with the Soviet Union.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: May I explain again. On 13 January Goering told me that Hitler had the impression that Russia intended to march against Germany. That was not Goering's opinion, neither was it mine. I assume it was Hitler's opinion which he had expressed as his own.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Excuse me. Do I understand that neither you nor Goering thought this opinion of Hitler's to be correct?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I can only speak for myself. I often expressed it as my view that Russia would not go against us. What Goering thought about it I could not say. He did not talk to me about it. You should ask him.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Yes, and now I shall ask you. You mean to say that you personally did not share Hitler's opinion? And you mean that Goering, too, did not want a war against the Soviet Union?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: On 22 May, when I spoke to Goering about this matter and urgently requested him to do everything to prevent a war with Russia, he told me that he had used the same arguments with Hitler but that it was impossible to get Hitler to change his mind; he had made his decision and no power on earth could influence him.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I see. You mean that Goering was opposed to a war with the Soviet Union, because he thought it impracticable while you were at war with England, and he wanted to prevent war on two fronts?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: From a purely military point of view, yes; and I believe that if war had been avoided at that time it would not have come about later.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: And you seriously maintain that it is possible to talk about a preventive war so far ahead, and at the same time to work out Case Barbarossa and all the directives to implement it, as well as gaining allies for the attack on Russia? Do you seriously believe in the preventive character of such a war?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I do not understand the meaning of the question.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Do you think one could make known that the Soviet Union was going to attack Germany, and at the same time work out an aggressive plan against the Soviet Union, and this as early as December 1940, as appears from the dates of the official documents?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: As I understand it, Hitler, expecting an attack by Russia -- if he really expected it -- said that he had to meet a Russian invasion by a preventive war. This, however, has nothing to do with the opinion for which I have been asked here. Speaking for myself, I did not unreservedly hold the view that Russia would invade us. Without being able to judge the situation as a whole, I personally believed that Russia in her own interest, which I tried to visualize, would not do this.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I understand. I should like to put a few questions to you with regard to the prisoners of war. The employment of prisoners of war, especially from the Soviet Union, on work in the aircraft industry has already been mentioned here.

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: What is your attitude to employing prisoners of war on work against their own country? What do you think of that?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: It is, of course, not a nice thing to do; but as far as I know it was also done to our prisoners of war by all the other countries.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I am talking of Germany now. You say it is not a nice thing. Is not that a rather mild way of putting it?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: It depends upon what the others do. All laws of warfare are based on reciprocity, as long as there is any reciprocity.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I should like you to answer my question. What was the German High Command's attitude to this kind of employment? Do you consider that by this employment the regulations of international law were being violated?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: That is a moot point which even now is not clear to me. I only know that orders were given to employ them, and to use these men, as well as women, in the struggle for our existence.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Do you consider this to be a legitimate order?

turns.speaker: MILCH
turns.text: I cannot judge that; that depends upon conditions and, as I said, upon reciprocity.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Mr. President, I ask to have this question and answer stricken from the record. The witness has been asked to give a legal opinion, and it is not for him to do so; since the question is not admissible, the answer too should be stricken.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: General Rudenko?

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: I should like to say I did not realize that the witness did not know whether or not this was a violation of international law. I had every reason to believe that the witness was competent to answer this question, the more so as at the beginning of his statement today, and on Friday, he mentioned the ten rules of the soldier, which he said must not be broken as they were based on international law. I thought, therefore, the witness to be competent to answer the question concerning the use of prisoners of war by the Luftwaffe against their own country. If the Tribunal considers this question to be inadmissible, I will of course withdraw it.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The question might have been framed differently, as to whether it was not a breach of the rules set out in the soldiers' pay book. However, as to international law, that is one of the matters which the Tribunal has got to decide, and upon that, of course, we do not wish the evidence of witnesses.

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: Yes. I still have two questions to put to this witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We wanted to rise at half-past 4. If it is your intention to ask some more questions, perhaps we had better rise now, or, have you finished?

turns.speaker: GEN. RUDENKO
turns.text: We had better call a recess now, because I may still have a few questions to put to this witness. [The Tribunal adjourned until 12 March 1946 at 1000 hours.]
